cataloging_subject.label: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies: selected vocabularies XIII–XX
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edition-level selection represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00034: the 1884 title page and eight numbered vocabularies, XIII–XX, at printed cols. 536–814 of Wright and Wülcker's volume I. The unit is neither one medieval composition nor the complete 1884 volume. Its components derive from seven manuscripts and include lexical prose/list structures, two metrical vocabularies, embedded verse, and illustrations.

catalog_summary.title: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies: selected vocabularies XIII–XX
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous medieval vocabulary compilers
catalog_summary.date: Components transmitted from the early thirteenth through the fifteenth/early sixteenth century; selected second-edition compilation published 1884
catalog_summary.region: England; component witnesses and dialect associations include Worcester, Reading, Shropshire, Lincolnshire, and tentative western-border and northern areas

titles.value: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies: selected vocabularies XIII–XX

assertion.notes: The supplied qualifier prevents the U-M item from being mistaken for the complete printed volume.

assertion.notes: This title belongs to the complete edition; CME00034 contains only nos. XIII–XX and the title page.

agents.name: Anonymous medieval vocabulary compilers
notes: A cataloging collective for separately anonymous components; it does not assert common authorship or one act of compilation.

agents.name: Thomas Wright
notes: 1810–1877; compiler, original editor, transcriber, and commentator of the 1857 collection. He is not the medieval author of the vocabularies.

agents.name: Richard Paul Wülcker
notes: 1845–1910; second-edition editor, compiler, reviser, collator, and commentator.

agents.name: William Aldis Wright
notes: 1831–1914; supplied a transcript of Trinity College Cambridge MS O.5.4 for vocabulary XV.

scope.description: Eight separately transmitted medieval lexical components

assertion.notes: Anonymous status does not imply shared authorship. The name Spencer at the end of XVI is treated as a possible copyist, not as established author.

form_statements.description: Prose/list vocabularies, two metrical vocabularies, and embedded verse and illustrations in later components
Lexical entries arranged as prose/list structures
Latin metrical vocabularies with English glossing
Predominantly lexical lists with encoded verse lines; XX also includes sixty-one encoded figures

summaries.value: An edition-level selection of eight anonymous medieval lexical texts, numbered XIII–XX in Richard Paul Wülcker's revised 1884 edition of Thomas Wright's vocabulary collection. The components range from an early Middle English abridgement of an Ælfrician glossary and a trilingual herb list to schoolroom metrical vocabularies, large nominalia, and an illustrated Latin–English word list. Together they document language teaching, material culture, lexical organization, dialect, and nineteenth-century manuscript editing.

The eight components are XIII A Semi-Saxon Vocabulary; XIV A Vocabulary of the Names of Plants; XV A Latin and English Vocabulary; XVI Metrical Vocabularies; XVII A Vocabulary of the Names of the Parts of the Human Body; XVIII An English Vocabulary; XIX A Nominale; and XX A Pictorial Vocabulary. XV includes an additions section, and XIX contains seven internal chapters.

CME00034 preserves the 1884 edition title page and the continuous printed run from col. 536 through col. 814, corresponding to complete scan leaves 302–446. It omits vocabularies I–XII, the edition's other preliminaries and index matter, and any volume beyond this selected run.

extent.description: Eight numbered DIV2 vocabulary components nested in a collection-level DIV1, plus a separate title-page DIV1. The selected text contains 9,548 ITEM elements; one additional ITEM belongs to administrative TEMPHEAD revision metadata. XML computation also finds 280 PB elements, 1,006 MILESTONE elements, 595 NOTE elements, 376 L elements, two LG elements, and sixty-one FIGURE elements. The prose/verse/mixed counts describe encoding form, not eight modern independent works.

content_parts.label: XIII. A Semi-Saxon Vocabulary
content_parts.summary: A fragmentary early Middle English abridgement/adaptation of the Ælfrician glossary tradition, surviving in binding leaves now Worcester Cathedral Library F.174.
notes: The historical label Semi-Saxon is retained as an edition title, not as a current language code.

content_parts.label: XIV. A Vocabulary of the Names of Plants
content_parts.summary: A trilingual Latin, Anglo-Norman, and Middle English list of herbs, likely made for practical or medical use.
notes: Current and historical foliation have not yet been reconciled.

content_parts.label: XV. A Latin and English Vocabulary
content_parts.summary: English equivalents selected from a much larger Latin dictionary, with an encoded additions section; printed from a transcript supplied by W. Aldis Wright.
notes: The manuscript span contains unselected Latin matter; it is not a one-to-one continuous transcript of every entry.
Trinity warns that modern foliation is incorrect but adopts it for reference.

content_parts.label: XVI. Metrical Vocabularies
content_parts.summary: Two Latin metrical school vocabularies, the Distigius and Bursa Latini, with Middle English glosses.
notes: The subscribed name Spencer may identify a copyist; authorship is not assigned.

content_parts.label: XVII. A Vocabulary of the Names of the Parts of the Human Body
content_parts.summary: A short Latin metrical list of body-part names with Middle English glosses, beginning Os facies mentum.

content_parts.label: XVIII. An English Vocabulary
content_parts.summary: A large Latin–Middle English nominalia beginning with the parts of the human body and extending through themed lexical fields.
notes: The current BL catalog's edition cross-reference starts at p./col. 635, while direct inspection shows XVIII begins at col. 633; the direct edition boundary is preferred.

content_parts.label: XIX. A Nominale
content_parts.summary: The Mayer Nominale, a large Latin–Middle English topical vocabulary in seven encoded chapters, transmitted in a manuscript formerly owned by Joseph Mayer.
notes: The edition records a missing/torn leaf; the textual lacuna is not silently reconstructed.

content_parts.label: XX. A Pictorial Vocabulary
content_parts.summary: A large Latin–Middle English vocabulary accompanied in its manuscript by numerous black and red marginal drawings; the encoding preserves sixty-one FIGURE markers.
notes: Beinecke f. 23 contains adjacent notes and letter formulae outside the work portion.

related_works.title: A Volume of Vocabularies
notes: Wülcker revised seven selected components from Wright's first edition; vocabulary XV was newly added and is excluded from a blanket identity claim.

related_works.title: Anglo-Saxon and Old English Vocabularies, volume I: Vocabularies
notes: CME00034 contains only the title page and vocabularies XIII–XX, cols. 536–814, not the whole volume.

related_works.title: Ælfric's Glossary
notes: Vocabulary XIII is described as an abridgement/adaptation in the Ælfrician glossary tradition; this does not make Ælfric the author of all selected wording.

open_questions.question: Which historical editorial dates for the eight component texts remain accepted in current lexical scholarship, independently of their witness dates?
next_steps: Consult current specialist editions and linguistic studies for each component.
Record composition, compilation, revision, and witness attestation as separate dated events.

open_questions.question: Which current folios of Worcester Cathedral Library F.174 correspond exactly to vocabulary XIII?
next_steps: Request a current folio concordance from Worcester Cathedral Library and compare it with the 1884 edition.
Avoid supplying an exact range until physical or catalog evidence resolves it.

open_questions.question: How does the edition's Harley 978 f. 24v locator correspond to the current catalog's ff. 26r–27v?
next_steps: Ask the British Library for foliation history or inspect a complete reproduction with both numerations visible.

open_questions.question: Should the historical western-border assignment for XVI–XVII and Northern(?) assignment for XVIII be refined or rejected under current dialectology?
next_steps: Compare the forms cited by Wright and Wülcker with current Linguistic Atlas and specialist vocabulary scholarship.

notes: Thomas Wright and Richard Wülcker are cataloged as editors/compilers of the nineteenth-century selection, not as medieval authors.
Text composition, witness copying, institutional provenance, dialect, printed edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, and digital facsimile are modeled as distinct layers.
The preferred title adds 'selected vocabularies XIII–XX' because the inherited U-M title alone could imply complete coverage of the 1884 volume.
The title-page phrase '[All rights reserved.]' is a historical 1884 publication statement and does not override the current U.S. public-domain rights evidence modeled in the lineage manifest.
